title: Loss Functions & Training Objectives
summary: Loss defines what 'correct' means. Cross-entropy for classification/generation, MSE for regression, contrastive for similarity. Choice of loss is an architectural decision — it determines output layer shape and what the model optimizes for.
key_insights: Loss choice = implicit architectural decision. CE needs softmax output layer, MSE needs linear, contrastive needs paired inputs.
Cross-entropy = forward KL divergence: $\text{CE}(p, q) = H(p) + D_{KL}(p \| q)$. Minimizing CE = matching distributions.
CE gradient w.r.t. logits is beautifully simple: $\nabla_{z_i} \text{CE} = q_i - y_i$ (predicted minus target). No second derivatives.
MSE penalizes large errors quadratically — sensitive to outliers. MAE (L1) is robust but not differentiable at 0.
Contrastive loss: learn representations by pushing similar pairs together, dissimilar apart. Temperature $\tau$ controls sharpness.
KL divergence is asymmetric: $D_{KL}(p\|q) \neq D_{KL}(q\|p)$. Forward KL = mode-covering, reverse KL = mode-seeking.

mathematical_core.cross_entropy: $$\text{CE}(y, q) = -\sum_i y_i \log q_i$$ where $y$ = one-hot target, $q$ = softmax output. For language modeling: $\text{CE} = -\log q_{\text{correct\_token}}$.
mathematical_core.perplexity: $\text{PPL} = e^{\text{CE}}$ — interpretable: 'how many tokens is the model confused between'

derivations.title: CE gradient simplicity — why softmax + CE is the standard pairing

steps.name: Gradient w.r.t. logits
steps.math: $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} = q_i - y_i$$ For correct class: $q_k - 1$ (push up). For wrong classes: $q_i - 0 = q_i$ (push down).

derivations.consequence: Gradient is simply (prediction − target). No saturation, no vanishing. This is why CE+softmax is universal for classification.

derivations.title: Temperature in contrastive loss

steps.name: Effect of $\tau$
steps.math: Small $\tau$: $e^{\text{sim}/\tau}$ amplifies differences → hard negatives dominate. Large $\tau$: all negatives contribute equally.

steps.name: Connection to distillation
steps.math: Same $\tau$ appears in knowledge distillation: $q_i = e^{z_i/\tau} / \sum_j e^{z_j/\tau}$. Higher $\tau$ = softer distribution = more information per sample.

derivations.consequence: Temperature is a universal knob: controls how peaked vs uniform the target/output distribution is. Appears in attention ($1/\sqrt{d_k}$), contrastive, distillation.

derivations.title: Why KL divergence is asymmetric — practical consequences

steps.name: Forward KL: $D_{KL}(p\|q)$
steps.math: $q$ must cover everywhere $p > 0$. If $p(x) > 0$ but $q(x) \approx 0$: $p \log(p/q) \to \infty$. Mode-covering: $q$ spreads out.

steps.name: Reverse KL: $D_{KL}(q\|p)$
steps.math: $q$ can ignore modes of $p$. If $q(x) > 0$ but $p(x) \approx 0$: $q \log(q/p) \to \infty$. Mode-seeking: $q$ concentrates.

derivations.consequence: Standard training uses forward KL (CE). VAE uses reverse KL (ELBO). GANs implicitly optimize JS divergence. Choice affects whether model hedges (forward) or commits (reverse).

geometric_properties: CE loss surface is convex w.r.t. softmax outputs (but not w.r.t. parameters)
MSE loss surface is a paraboloid in output space — symmetric, single minimum
Contrastive loss creates a hypersphere of embeddings where similar items cluster
statistical_properties: CE on balanced dataset: random model gives $\text{CE} = \log V$ (perplexity = $V$)
Training CE should decrease monotonically. Val CE turning up = overfitting.
Label smoothing: replace one-hot $y_k = 1$ with $y_k = 1 - \epsilon$, others $= \epsilon/(V-1)$. Prevents overconfident logits.
numerical_properties: LogSumExp trick: $\log \sum e^{z_i} = z_{\max} + \log \sum e^{z_i - z_{\max}}$ prevents overflow.
float16: CE values > 65504 overflow. Use loss scaling for mixed precision.
notes: Custom loss functions for specific business objectives is a key skill for application design.
